articles.title: America’s 2026 election system is already in court, and the early fight is over who gets to write the rules
articles.summary: From Wisconsin’s judiciary to Trump’s election order and a pending Supreme Court ballot case, the 2026 cycle is being shaped by a rapid, nationwide clash over election administration before a single House seat is decided.

articles.title: Arts Institutions Enter 2026 With Fewer Safety Nets and More Pressure to Prove Their Value
articles.summary: New federal grant rules, shrinking local budgets, rising costs and audience shifts are forcing museums, theaters and other cultural institutions to rethink who they serve, how they pay workers and what they can still afford to do.

articles.title: Inside the bottleneck powering the AI boom: data centers are outrunning grids, chips and policy
articles.summary: A wave of new data-center announcements is colliding with electricity constraints, equipment shortages and tougher trade policy, forcing a reordering of the AI build-out.

articles.title: Tariffs, gasoline and shelter are squeezing household budgets again
articles.summary: A March inflation shock is colliding with rising import costs, sticky housing expenses and a labor market that is still growing paychecks — just not fast enough for many families.

articles.title: FAFSA overhaul, attendance recovery, and the fight to keep students connected
articles.summary: As states and districts enter the final stretch of the 2025-26 school year, education leaders are being forced to tackle three intertwined crises at once: unstable federal aid rules, stubborn chronic absenteeism, and deep staffing shortages that keep schools from offering the consistency families need.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: FAFSA overhaul, attendance recovery, and the fight to keep students connected. AI-generated illustration by The Press.

articles.title: Americas measles surge tests fragile vaccine gains as U.S. elimination review looms
articles.summary: A fast-moving measles resurgence across the Americas is colliding with uneven vaccination coverage, strained surveillance and a U.S. elimination-status review that could reverberate through public health policy and travel medicine.

articles.title: Schools Are Teaching with AI Before We’ve Agreed on What School Is For
articles.summary: Generative AI has moved from novelty to infrastructure in classrooms, but the deeper crisis is philosophical: if schools can no longer tell the difference between assistance and authorship, they must decide whether education is about performance, understanding, or becoming a certain kind of person.

articles.title: WHO’s One Health push meets a harsher reality: the science is stronger than the system
articles.summary: A global health summit in Lyon put One Health back at the center of public science diplomacy — but the harder question is whether countries will fund the surveillance, labs and cross-border coordination needed to make it work.

articles.title: Why April 2026 is becoming a stress test for migration governance from the Sahel to South Asia
articles.summary: From Chad’s refugee camps to the Afghan-Pakistani border and the EU’s June asylum overhaul, states are tightening borders just as displacement, aid cuts, and cross-border insecurity are intensifying.

articles.title: America Should Stop Treating Housing Affordability Like a Buzzword and Start Treating It Like Infrastructure
articles.summary: The new federal housing push is welcome but incomplete. The only durable way to make homes cheaper is to build far more of them, faster, in the places people actually want to live—and to stop confusing financing gimmicks with supply reform.

articles.title: AI’s power problem is no longer a forecast. It is a policy fight now.
articles.summary: As data centers, grid planners, regulators, and chipmakers collide, the AI boom is increasingly being shaped by a less glamorous constraint: electricity. The question is no longer whether AI will transform the economy, but who pays for the power, where the plants go, and how much of the burden falls on households, workers, and public institutions.

articles.title: Lebanon’s fragile pause is turning into a test of deterrence, sovereignty and great-power patience
articles.summary: A 10-day ceasefire, direct Israel-Lebanon talks, and attacks on UN peacekeepers have exposed how regional wars now hinge on logistics, alliances and the narrow space between battlefield gains and diplomatic collapse.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: Lebanon’s fragile pause is turning into a test of deterrence, sovereignty and great-power patience. AI-generated illustration by The Press.

articles.title: CinemaCon 2026 puts Hollywood’s theatrical future on trial
articles.summary: At this year’s theater-owner confab in Las Vegas, studio chiefs are promising more movies, firmer windows and a reset for a battered exhibition business — but the box office, labor pipeline and production geography still suggest the recovery is fragile.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: CinemaCon 2026 puts Hollywood’s theatrical future on trial. AI-generated illustration by The Press.

articles.title: AI is flooding music platforms, and the pop economy is changing fast
articles.summary: From Deezer’s daily flood of synthetic uploads to platform labeling experiments and new licensing fights, 2026 is forcing the music business to redraw the line between discovery, fraud, and authorship.

articles.title: The 60-Meter Renaissance: Inside Amateur Radio’s New HF Frontier
articles.summary: A recent FCC rule change opened a fresh slice of 5 MHz to U.S. amateurs in February 2026, and the hobby’s technical corner is using it to argue for relevance, resilience, and a different idea of community.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial illustration for: The 60-Meter Renaissance: Inside Amateur Radio’s New HF Frontier. AI-generated illustration by The Press.
